(also known as **Ariola Records**, **Ariola Express**, **Ariola-Eurodisc** and **BMG Ariola**) is a German [record label](/wiki/Record_label "Record label"). In the late 1980s, it was a subsidiary label of the [Bertelsmann Music Group](/wiki/Bertelsmann_Music_Group "Bertelsmann Music Group"), which in turn has become a part of the international media conglomerate [Sony Music Entertainment](/wiki/Sony_Music "Sony Music").
["Ariola Sets Sight on Leading Phono Output in Common Market"](https://books.google.com/books?id=QUUEAAAAMBAJ&dq=Ariola+Eurodisc+GmbH&pg=PA6). *Billboard*. 15 August 1964. p. 6.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Ariola Records](/w/index.php?title=Ariola_Records&redirect=no "Ariola Records"))

German record label

This article is about the German record label. For the anatomical structure, see [Areola](/wiki/Areola "Areola").

**Ariola** (also known as **Ariola Records**, **Ariola Express**, **Ariola-Eurodisc** and **BMG Ariola**) is a German [record label](/wiki/Record_label "Record label"). In the late 1980s, it was a subsidiary label of the [Bertelsmann Music Group](/wiki/Bertelsmann_Music_Group "Bertelsmann Music Group"), which in turn has become a part of the international media conglomerate [Sony Music Entertainment](/wiki/Sony_Music "Sony Music").

## Profile

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ariola&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Profile")]

Ariola Eurodisc GmbH was founded in 1958 as a music outlet of [Bertelsmann](/wiki/Bertelsmann "Bertelsmann"). It set up several foreign subsidiaries. Leveraging acquisitions by its parent company, Ariola positioned itself to become a strong contender in the German record industry in the mid-1960s.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Ariola America was founded in 1975 in Los Angeles, and achieved *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_magazine "Billboard magazine")* magazine number one singles with [Mary MacGregor](/wiki/Mary_MacGregor "Mary MacGregor")'s "[Torn Between Two Lovers](/wiki/Torn_Between_Two_Lovers_(song) "Torn Between Two Lovers (song)")" (1976) and [Amii Stewart](/wiki/Amii_Stewart "Amii Stewart")'s cover version of the 1966 [Eddie Floyd](/wiki/Eddie_Floyd "Eddie Floyd") hit "[Knock on Wood](/wiki/Knock_on_Wood_(Eddie_Floyd_song) "Knock on Wood (Eddie Floyd song)")" (1979). Other artists on the Ariola America roster during the late 1970s included [Gene Cotton](/wiki/Gene_Cotton "Gene Cotton"), [The Three Degrees](/wiki/The_Three_Degrees "The Three Degrees"), Chanson, and the Canadian band [Prism](/wiki/Prism_(band) "Prism (band)") among others. After its pop success dried up, Ariola America found success in the Spanish language market including [José José](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Jos%C3%A9 "José José") and [Rocío Dúrcal](/wiki/Roc%C3%ADo_D%C3%BArcal "Rocío Dúrcal") from the late 1970s onward and Mexican artist [Marisela](/wiki/Marisela "Marisela") starting in the 1990s. Other subsidiaries include Ariola-Athena to release spoken word records; Ariola Benelux was founded in 1970 to cover the [Benelux](/wiki/Benelux "Benelux") market; Ariola Eurodisc S.A. was a Spanish division of Ariola, founded in 1970; Ariola UK, the UK based subsidiary of Ariola, was founded in 1977; Baccarola; Türküola, a subsidiary for the Turkish market; [Eurodisc](/wiki/Eurodisc "Eurodisc") to release classical records in Europe and Arabella to release disco records in France - most successfully with [Amanda Lear](/wiki/Amanda_Lear "Amanda Lear"), [Giorgio Moroder](/wiki/Giorgio_Moroder "Giorgio Moroder") (house producer of Ariola); and Jupiter Records for German [Schlager music](/wiki/Schlager_music "Schlager music") and disco artists such as [Dschinghis Khan](/wiki/Dschinghis_Khan "Dschinghis Khan").

It acquired both [Arista Records](/wiki/Arista_Records "Arista Records") and German rival label [Hansa Records](/wiki/Hansa_Records "Hansa Records") in 1979. It sold 50% of Arista to [RCA Records](/wiki/RCA_Records "RCA Records") in 1983; after a brief joint venture between RCA and Ariola, RCA (including its record division) was acquired by [General Electric](/wiki/General_Electric "General Electric") who promptly sold RCA's stake in the venture back to Ariola. Ariola America also had a sister label called Ocean Records, which included on its roster actress/entertainer [Ann-Margret](/wiki/Ann-Margret "Ann-Margret"). It was active 1979 through 1980. In the 1980s, there was a software development division called [Ariolasoft](/wiki/Ariolasoft "Ariolasoft").

Today, Ariola is a unit of [Sony Music Group](/wiki/Sony_Music_Group "Sony Music Group") and is still active in Germany under the Sony Group Corporation umbrella.
